subUnits.title: 1.1 Characteristics of Living Things
notes: Characteristics of Living Things:
Living organisms share certain fundamental characteristics that distinguish them from non-living things. These characteristics are often remembered using the acronym MR NIGER D.
The Seven Characteristics of Living Things:
1. Movement:
• Definition: Ability to change position or location
• In animals: Locomotion using muscles, limbs, wings, fins
• In plants: Growth movements (tropisms), nastic movements
• Nigerian example: Birds migrating, roots growing toward water
2. Respiration:
• Definition: Chemical process of releasing energy from food
• Aerobic respiration: Uses oxygen, produces CO₂, water, and energy
• Anaerobic respiration: Occurs without oxygen (fermentation)
• Nigerian example: Yeast fermentation in local brewing (burukutu)
3. Nutrition:
• Definition: Obtaining and using food for growth and energy
• Autotrophic nutrition: Making own food (photosynthesis in plants)
• Heterotrophic nutrition: Consuming other organisms (animals, fungi)
• Nigerian example: Cassava plants making food via photosynthesis; humans eating varied diets
4. Irritability (Sensitivity):
• Definition: Ability to detect and respond to stimuli
• In animals: Nervous and endocrine systems
• In plants: Tropisms (phototropism, geotropism), nastic movements
• Nigerian example: Mimosa pudica leaves folding when touched
5. Growth:
• Definition: Permanent increase in size, mass, or number of cells
• Involves cell division (mitosis) and cell enlargement
• Nigerian example: Growth of maize plants from seed to harvest
6. Excretion:
• Definition: Removal of metabolic waste products
• In animals: Kidneys (urine), lungs (CO₂), skin (sweat)
• In plants: Stomata (O₂, water vapor), leaf fall (waste accumulation)
• Nigerian example: Human kidneys filtering waste; plants shedding old leaves
7. Reproduction:
• Definition: Production of new individuals of the same species
• Asexual reproduction: One parent, offspring genetically identical
• Sexual reproduction: Two parents, genetic variation in offspring
• Nigerian example: Cassava propagation by stem cuttings (asexual); human reproduction (sexual)
Difference Between Plants and Animals:
1. Nutrition:
• Plants: Autotrophic (make their own food through photosynthesis)
• Animals: Heterotrophic (depend on plants or other animals for food)
2. Movement:
• Plants: Limited to growth movements and responses to stimuli
• Animals: Can move freely from place to place (locomotion)
3. Growth:
• Plants: Indefinite growth throughout life; growth occurs at meristems
• Animals: Definite growth period; growth occurs throughout body
4. Cell Structure:
• Plants: Have cell walls made of cellulose; have chloroplasts; large central vacuole
• Animals: No cell walls; no chloroplasts; small temporary vacuoles
5. Response to Stimuli:
• Plants: Slower response; use hormones (auxins)
• Animals: Rapid response; use nervous and endocrine systems
6. Excretion:
• Plants: Simple excretory products; no specialized excretory organs
• Animals: Complex nitrogenous wastes; specialized excretory organs (kidneys)
7. Reproduction:
• Plants: Both sexual and asexual reproduction common
• Animals: Mostly sexual reproduction; asexual rare (except lower animals)
Levels of Organization of Life:
Living organisms are organized in a hierarchical manner from simple to complex:
1. Cell:
• Basic unit of life
• Examples: Red blood cell, nerve cell, leaf cell
• Nigerian context: Cells in cassava leaves performing photosynthesis
2. Tissue:
• Group of similar cells performing a specific function
• Examples: Muscle tissue, nervous tissue, xylem tissue
• Nigerian context: Xylem tissue transporting water in maize stems
3. Organ:
• Structure composed of multiple tissue types performing a complex function
• Examples: Heart, liver, leaf, root
• Nigerian context: Yam tubers as storage organs
4. Organ System:
• Group of organs working together for a major function
• Examples: Digestive system, circulatory system, root system
• Nigerian context: Human digestive system processing local foods
5. Organism:
• Complete living individual
• Examples: Human being, cassava plant, goat
• Nigerian context: A complete yam plant growing in a farm
Practical Activities:
1. Group Activity: Living vs Non-living Things
• Teacher groups students into two: one group collects living things, the other collects non-living things
• Students compare and contrast their collections
• Discussion: What characteristics do living things share?
2. Observation Activity: Cockroach vs Potted Plant
• Teacher provides a living cockroach and a potted plant
• Students observe and write down their observations
• Discussion: How do these two organisms differ in their characteristics?
Common Misconceptions:
• 'Plants don't move': Plants show growth movements and responses to stimuli
• 'Only animals respire': All living things respire to release energy
• 'Growth is only increase in size': Growth includes cell division and differentiation
• 'Excretion is only urination': Excretion includes removal of all metabolic wastes
Nigerian Context:
• Understanding life characteristics helps Nigerian farmers optimize crop and livestock production
• Knowledge of respiration and nutrition informs public health campaigns on diet and disease
• Recognizing adaptations aids conservation efforts for Nigeria's unique biodiversity
Conclusion:
• The characteristics of living things provide a framework for understanding life
• These characteristics are interconnected and essential for survival
• Mastery of these concepts is foundational for all biological studies
• In Nigeria, applying this knowledge supports health, agriculture, and conservation goals

quiz.q: The ability to detect and respond to stimuli is called:
quiz.explanation: Irritability (or sensitivity) is the ability of organisms to detect changes in their environment and respond appropriately.

quiz.q: Respiration in living things primarily involves:
quiz.explanation: Respiration is the chemical process of breaking down food to release energy, which may or may not involve oxygen.

quiz.q: An example of excretion in plants is:
quiz.explanation: Plants excrete waste products through processes like leaf fall, where accumulated wastes are shed with old leaves.

quiz.q: Growth in living organisms is best defined as:
opts: Permanent increase in size, mass, or cell number
quiz.explanation: Growth involves permanent increases through cell division, enlargement, and differentiation.

quiz.q: A major difference between plants and animals is that plants:
quiz.explanation: Plants are autotrophic, making their own food through photosynthesis, while animals are heterotrophic.

quiz.q: The basic unit of life is the:
quiz.explanation: The cell is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

opts: A group of similar cells performing a specific function
quiz.explanation: Tissues are groups of similar cells that work together to perform a particular function.

quiz.q: In Nigerian agriculture, understanding characteristics of life helps farmers to:
quiz.explanation: Knowledge of life processes helps farmers provide optimal conditions for growth, reproduction, and productivity.

quiz.q: A common misconception about living things is:
quiz.explanation: All living things respire to release energy, not just animals. Plants and other organisms also respire.

quiz.q: The level of organization that comes after tissue is:
quiz.explanation: The hierarchical organization is: cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism.
